subject: Aramean military casualties in David's wars

figures.name: Aramean charioteers killed
figures.note: All textual traditions consistently report 700 charioteers killed in verse 18.

figures.name: Aramean footmen killed
figures.note: All traditions uniformly attest 40,000 footmen killed in verse 18.

systematic_analysis.pattern: No numerical divergence exists between MT, LXX, and SP in 2 Samuel 10. All three textual traditions report identical figures: 700 charioteers and 40,000 footmen killed. However, there is a well-known inner-biblical divergence with the parallel account in 1 Chronicles 19:18, which reports 7,000 charioteers instead of 700—a discrepancy that likely reflects a scribal error involving Hebrew numerals rather than systematic textual tradition differences.
systematic_analysis.pattern_plain: All three major textual traditions agree perfectly on the numbers in 2 Samuel 10, though a different number appears in the parallel account in Chronicles.

theories.name: Independent Textual Stability
theories.summary: The perfect agreement among MT, LXX, and SP suggests that this numerical data was firmly established before the major textual traditions diverged. The agreement across all three independent transmission streams indicates the numbers were preserved accurately from a common Vorlage. This pattern contrasts sharply with passages like Genesis 5 and 11 where systematic divergences appear, suggesting different transmission dynamics for narrative military statistics versus chronological data.
theories.summary_plain: All three textual traditions independently preserved the same numbers, indicating these figures were already fixed before the traditions separated.
supporting_evidence: Perfect numerical agreement across three independent textual families (MT, LXX, SP) is statistically significant for this type of data
The stability suggests military statistics in narrative contexts were less susceptible to theological manipulation than chronological data
Ancient versions (Vulgate, Targums, Peshitta) uniformly support the 700/40,000 figures
The divergence appears only in the inner-biblical parallel (Chronicles), not across textual families
Military casualty figures typically show high stability in transmission compared to genealogical or chronological numbers
weaknesses: Does not explain the 700 vs 7,000 divergence with 1 Chronicles 19:18
Cannot account for why some numerical passages show stability while others (Genesis chronologies) show systematic variation
The theory is essentially negative, explaining only what did not happen rather than accounting for transmission mechanisms

theories.name: Scribal Error in Chronicles Tradition
theories.summary: The discrepancy between 2 Samuel 10:18 (700 charioteers) and 1 Chronicles 19:18 (7,000 charioteers) is best explained by confusion of Hebrew numerical notation: ת״ש (700) versus ז׳ אלפים (7,000). This is a classic example of paleographic confusion rather than systematic textual tradition divergence. The error occurred within the Hebrew transmission of Samuel-Chronicles, not across the LXX/MT/SP divide, which is why all three major traditions agree for 2 Samuel 10.
theories.summary_plain: The difference with Chronicles is likely a simple copying mistake involving Hebrew number notation, not a systematic pattern across textual traditions.
supporting_evidence: Hebrew numerical notation was particularly prone to confusion between hundreds and thousands in certain scribal contexts
The error pattern (tenfold increase) matches known paleographic confusion in alphabetic numerals
All three major textual traditions agreeing on 2 Samuel while Chronicles diverges indicates the error entered the Chronicles stream specifically
Parallel accounts in Samuel-Kings versus Chronicles frequently show minor numerical discrepancies attributable to independent copying
No theological motivation is apparent for either figure, suggesting mechanical error rather than intentional change
weaknesses: Some scholars argue Chronicles may preserve an independent tradition rather than simply containing a scribal error
Does not explain why the Chronicler's figure was not corrected by later scribes aware of the Samuel parallel
Assumes Chronicles is secondary to Samuel rather than potentially drawing on independent sources

theories.name: MT Deflation
theories.summary: This theory, which proposes systematic reduction of numbers in the MT tradition to make figures more historically plausible, finds no support in 2 Samuel 10. All three textual traditions agree on the numbers, indicating no deflation occurred in the MT stream. The theory fails to account for the actual textual evidence in this passage, where MT shows no tendency toward lower figures compared to LXX or SP.
theories.summary_plain: The theory that the Masoretic Text systematically reduced numbers does not apply here, since all traditions have identical figures.
supporting_evidence: The theory has been proposed for passages like Genesis 5 and 11 chronologies but lacks applicability to 2 Samuel 10
weaknesses: Completely fails to explain the perfect agreement across all three textual traditions
No evidence of MT presenting lower figures than LXX or SP in this passage
The theory was developed for chronological data, not military casualty figures, and its extension here is unjustified
Ignores the actual textual data which shows no MT deflation pattern

theories.name: LXX Inflation
theories.summary: The theory that LXX systematically inflated numbers to enhance theological themes or emphasize divine intervention finds no support in 2 Samuel 10. The LXX presents identical figures to MT and SP (700 charioteers, 40,000 footmen), showing no tendency toward inflation. This passage provides counter-evidence to any universal LXX inflation hypothesis.
theories.summary_plain: The theory that the Septuagint systematically increased numbers is not supported here, as it matches the other traditions exactly.
supporting_evidence: The theory has limited support even in passages where it has been proposed (Genesis chronologies)
weaknesses: LXX shows no inflation whatsoever in 2 Samuel 10, contradicting the theory
Perfect agreement with MT and SP indicates no systematic revision in the Greek translation process
The theory cannot account for selective application—why inflate Genesis numbers but not military statistics
No manuscript evidence supports LXX presenting higher figures than MT for this passage

theories.name: SP Harmonization
theories.summary: The Samaritan Pentateuch is known for harmonizing parallel passages and resolving discrepancies, but this theory is irrelevant to 2 Samuel 10, which lies outside the Pentateuchal corpus. SP has no textual witness for Samuel. The theory cannot be evaluated for this passage as no SP version exists.
theories.summary_plain: This theory cannot apply because the Samaritan Pentateuch only covers Genesis through Deuteronomy, not 2 Samuel.
supporting_evidence: SP harmonization is well-documented within the Pentateuch but has no bearing on books outside that corpus
weaknesses: Theory is completely inapplicable to 2 Samuel, which falls outside the SP textual tradition
No SP manuscript evidence exists for the book of Samuel
The question is based on a methodological error in assuming SP relevance for non-Pentateuchal material
Should receive zero weight in evaluation of this passage

theories.name: Theological Motivation
theories.summary: Theologically motivated alterations of numbers to emphasize divine power, fulfill typological patterns, or adjust chronological frameworks are not evident in 2 Samuel 10. The perfect agreement across traditions suggests no theological agenda drove numerical changes. Military casualty figures lack the theological sensitivity of chronological or genealogical data, making them less likely targets for motivated revision.
theories.summary_plain: There is no evidence that religious motivations caused changes to these numbers, as all traditions agree and the figures lack obvious theological significance.
supporting_evidence: Military statistics in narrative contexts typically lack the theological freight of chronological or genealogical numbers
No apparent theological advantage to either increasing or decreasing the casualty figures
The agreement across confessionally distinct communities (Jewish MT, Christian LXX, Samaritan SP) indicates no sectarian theological agenda
weaknesses: Cannot explain why some numerical passages show theologically motivated changes while others remain stable
The theory has more plausibility for the Chronicles parallel, where Chronicler's theological interests might have influenced transmission
Does not account for the transmission mechanisms that preserved agreement across traditions

overall_assessment: 2 Samuel 10 presents a striking case of numerical stability across all three major textual traditions (MT, LXX, SP), with perfect agreement on 700 charioteers and 40,000 footmen killed. This unanimity suggests the figures were firmly fixed before the major textual families diverged, likely reflecting the high transmission fidelity of narrative military statistics compared to chronological data. The only significant divergence appears in the inner-biblical parallel (1 Chronicles 19:18, reporting 7,000 charioteers), which is best explained by paleographic scribal error in Hebrew numerical notation rather than systematic textual tradition differences. This passage provides important counter-evidence to theories of systematic MT deflation or LXX inflation, demonstrating that such patterns, if they exist, are not universal across all numerical data in the Hebrew Bible.
overall_plain: All three major ancient versions of 2 Samuel 10 agree perfectly on the numbers, showing these figures were accurately preserved. The only difference appears in a parallel passage in Chronicles, likely due to a simple copying error rather than intentional change.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Numerical Stability in Narrative Contradicts Systematic Revision
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The perfect cross-traditional agreement in 2 Samuel 10 demonstrates that theories of systematic numerical revision (MT deflation, LXX inflation) cannot be universally applied to all biblical numerical data. The stability of military casualty figures in narrative contexts contrasts sharply with the systematic divergences in Genesis chronologies, suggesting that scribal attitudes toward numerical data varied by literary genre and theological sensitivity. This selective pattern indicates that textual criticism must account for genre-specific transmission dynamics rather than proposing universal revision theories.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The fact that all ancient versions agree here but differ dramatically in other passages shows that scribes treated different types of numbers differently—they were more careful with battle statistics than with dates.
